paper_title: Regret Minimization with Adaptive Opponents in Repeated Games

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with \emph{adaptive} opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of \emph{external regret} in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players' counterfactual reasoning, we introduce {\tt Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)}, a game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the \emph{realized} and the \emph{best-in-hindsight} accumulated utility when all players can \emph{respond} to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining {\tt RP-Regret} sublinear in time, on the variation of the player's comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents' strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize {\tt RP-Regret}, which is by definition \emph{non-convex} in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes a convex and \emph{linearized} surrogate of {\tt RP-Regret} at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes {\tt RP-Regret} when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the {\tt RP-Regret} (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

sections.content: Mingyang Liu1 Asuman Ozdaglar1 Tiancheng Yu2 Kaiqing Zhang3 1 Massachusetts Institute of Technology 2 OpenAI 3 University of Maryland, College Park {liumy19,asuman}@mit.edu yutc14@gmail.com kaiqing@umd.edu Abstract In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with adaptive opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of external regret in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players counterfactual reasoning, we introduce Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret), game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the realized and the best-in-hindsight accumulated utility when all players can respond to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining RP-Regret sublinear in time, on the variation of the players comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize RP-Regret, which is by definition non-convex in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes convex and linearized surrogate of RP-Regret at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes RP-Regret when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the RP-Regret (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Solving for equilibria has long been one of the core problems in algorithmic game theory. However, an equilibrium may not correspond to solution that yields high utility2 for all the players in the game. For example, in the well-known game of Prisoners Dilemma (PD), the only Nash equilibrium (NE) is defect-defect, which yields relatively low utility for both players. Interestingly, however, when it comes to Iterated Prisoners Dilemma (IPD) (Axelrod and Hamilton, 1981), repeated version of PD, there exists an NE with much higher utility for both players (cf. ExamAlphabetical Order 1Accepted for presentation at the Conference on Learning Theory (COLT) 2026. 2For the classical examples of (Iterated) Prisoners Dilemma and Stag-Hunt, we follow the convention of using the term utility; later when defining regret notions, we will follow the convention from online learning and use the term loss throughout. 1 ple 1.1). Therefore, solving for equilibria in repeated games may have advantages over that in one-shot matrix games, in terms of possibly achieving higher utilities. More importantly, repeated games can also be employed to compute equilibria for one-shot In particular, one of the most efficient and scalable approaches for equilibrium comgames. putation is through no-regret learning/regret-minimization, which has been both theoretically (CesaBianchi and Lugosi, 2006; Roughgarden, 2010) and empirically (Brown and Sandholm, 2019, 2018) supported. It is known that when all players in the game run no-regret algorithm, their average strategy over time will converge to certain equilibria determined by the regret they minimize. At the heart of these approaches is proper definition of regret, which by default usually means external regret (Hannan, 1957; Hart and Mas-Colell, 2000), and measures the difference between the loss incurred by an online decision-maker (or player in repeated games) and certain comparator decisions that they would have made, knowing the time-varying environments in hindsight. However, in this classical regret definition, the loss sequences are implicitly assumed to be only functions of timestep (de Farias and Megiddo, 2003). This is general enough and perfectly sensible in the online learning setting, but not in game-theoretic one, where, by definition, the opponent players should be responsive to the players behavior during learning (Schlag and Zapechelnyuk, 2012). Hence, the classical regret cannot capture the adaptivity of the opponents, who can make decisions based on the history of the play, and the fact that players action may affect the opponents decisions later. Such an inability may lead to sub-optimal equilibrium solutions (e.g., always defect for IPD, as in Example 1.1). In fact, it has been shown that when players are responsive, achieving no-(external-)regret is impossible (Schlag and Zapechelnyuk, 2012). To account for the responsiveness and adaptivity of the opponents/adversaries, several new notions of regret have been developed for both online learning and repeated game settings. However, they are either not directly applicable to the repeated-game setting, or computationally intractable. For example, in the online learning setting, Merhav et al. (2002); Arora et al. (2012) introduced the notion of Policy Regret to model environments with memory, in which the loss at round may depend on history of past actions rather than only the current action. Their guarantees typically rely on an m-bounded-memory assumption, meaning that the loss at time depends only on the most recent actions. This assumption can be too restrictive in repeated games: deviation in an early round (e.g., at = 1) may alter the opponents observations later, and thereby change their subsequent play well beyond rounds, even when each player uses finite-memory strategy. In the repeated-game setting, Zinkevich (2005) proposed the notion of Response Regret, which considered the mixed strategy space that depends on the whole history of the play, making the minimization of Response Regret convex. However, this is at the cost of exponentially large space in the history length to store the strategies, and thus computationally intractable. Subsequently, Arora et al. (2018) extended the Policy Regret notion in Arora et al. (2012) to the game setting. However, the comparator in the regret considered there is restricted to constant actions and thus neither adaptive nor dynamic. Moreover, to achieve sublinear policy regret, Arora et al. (2018) assumed that the opponents strategies are insensitive to recent histories, which further restricted the adaptability and thus the power of opponents. For more detailed related work discussion, we refer to Appendix B. Motivated by these prior studies, our paper focuses on the repeated-game setting, and aims at developing regret notion that is natural and native to such setting, while retaining as much generality as possible, e.g., allowing time-varying/dynamic comparator strategies and few constraints on all players strategy spaces, while maintaining computational tractability and the possibility of finding better equilibrium solutions by minimizing such notion. We summarize 2 our contributions as follows. Our Contributions. Our main contributions are four-fold: (i) For repeated games, we advocate new and natural notion of Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret), as performance metric that measures the difference between the realized and the best-in-hindsight accumulated utility when all players can respond to the history of play. It allows the opponents to be adaptive, and the comparator strategies to be time-varying, i.e., dynamic; (ii) We prove series of necessary conditions for obtaining sublinear RP-Regret, on the variation of the players comparator strategies in the regret definition, as well as the memories of both the comparator and the opponents strategies (cf. Table 1); (iii) In light of the non-convexity in minimizing RP-Regret, we propose three algorithms: (1) one that is based on certain optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (2) one that minimizes linearized surrogate of RP-Regret at each timestep, Local Repeated Policy Regret (LRP-Regret), that is convex; (3) one that directly minimizes RP-Regret when the opponents change strategies slowly; (iv) We establish the relationship between the minimization of our regret notions and the computation of certain equilibria of the repeated game. Additionally, we also provide experimental results to demonstrate the advantage of our new regret notions in finding better cooperative solutions with higher utility for the players in games like the Stag-Hunt. Challenges & Our Techniques. Our notion of RP-Regret is general and particularly devised for the repeated-game setting, but at the cost of having non-convex objective to minimize due to the memory of players strategies. To address the non-convexity issue, we develop several approaches for RP-Regret minimization: (i) we resort to certain non-convex optimization oracles as in Suggala and Netrapalli (2020); (ii) we linearize the expected loss at each timestep to convexify it; (iii) we lift the variable dimension by reformulating the repeated game as Markov game, yielding convex objective in the occupancy measure space for the regret-minimizer. In particular, for (iii) we have developed new techniques to address the following challenges: Firstly, the occupancy measure by nature incorporates the strategies of both the regret-minimizer and the opponents in the game, which does not align with our setting where the opponents are not controlled by the regret-minimizer. Therefore, we need to optimize the occupancy measure while keeping the opponents strategies extracted from the occupancy measure close to the actual ones. Due to the online nature of game-playing, we cannot know the opponents strategies at timestep before we propose the occupancy measure at timestep t. Naively projecting to the occupancy measure space corresponding to the opponents strategies at the previous timestep 1 will cause the error to accumulate and eventually blow up. As result, we carefully design constraints for the occupancy measure, which can provably keep the first-order difference between the extracted opponents strategies and the actual ones bounded, and then resort to the framework of online learning with time-varying constraints to solve it; Secondly, we convert the violation of constraints during online learning to the RP-Regret upper bound; Thirdly, the original constraint on the forgetfulness of the behavioral-form strategies is non-convex with respect to the occupancy measure, addressing which requires us to redesign the constraint for the behavioral-form strategies. Motivating Examples. Now we present two examples to illustrate the advantages of our RP-Regret and LRP-Regret over the classical notion of external regret in playing repeated games. Due to space constraints, Example 2 can be found in A. 3 Example 1.1 (Existence of Better Strategy that is No-RP-Regret). In Iterated Prisoners Dilemma (Axelrod and Hamilton, 1981) (cf. utility matrix in Figure 1), when both players are minimizing the external regret (i.e., obtaining regret sublinear in time), the only strategy that the time-average strategies converge to is defect-defect (the only CCE of PD, since defect is strictly dominant strategy for both players), with utility of 0.2 for each player. Although the well-known tit-for-tat strategy (starting with Cooperate and mimicking the opponents action in the previous round) is an NE of the IPD with infinite rounds and enjoys higher time-average utility of 0.6, it, however, suffers linear external regret (cf. Appendix C). In contrast, when both players always play tit-for-tat, they will enjoy sublinear regret in terms of our new regret notion, RP-Regret (to be formally defined in 3), with the higher time-average utility (of 0.6) (cf. Appendix C). In other words, our RP-Regret can better capture this effective and cooperationpromoting strategy of tit-for-tat in this case, than the classical external regret.

sections.title: 3 A New Metric: Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)
sections.content: 3.1 RP-Regret Definition We consider the case in repeated games where the opponents are adaptive, i.e., they can make decisions by responding to histories of play. sections.title: 3.2 When is Minimizing RP-Regret Possible?
sections.content: Being fairly natural and native to repeated game playing, the notion of RP-Regret can be hard to minimize. To understand its fundamental limits, we first identify two basic conditions that can be proved necessary for achieving RP-Regret sublinear in T. The first one is variation condition, which restricts the comparators strategy from changing too fast across different timesteps. The second one concerns the imperfect memories of all the players and the comparator. (i) Condition 1 (Sublinear Variation). For given player , we say that the strategy π 1:T of player changes slowly with sublinear variation, when there exists constant [0, 1) such that O(T p). However, given the context of this paper, we will only discuss the adaptive regret for repeated games. Condition 1 on the comparator is common assumption in the literature of dynamic regret minimization (Zinkevich, 2003; Zhang et al., 2018a,b; Zhao et al., 2022), which restricts the power of the comparator in order to achieve sublinear dynamic regret. Note that the well-known external regret also implicitly adopts such condition with the variation of the comparator being zero. Condition 2 (Imperfect Recall (Piccione and Rubinstein, 1997; Waugh et al., 2009)). For given player , we say that player has imperfect recall, when they cannot remember all their histories perfectly. In particular, player cannot perfectly differentiate the observed histories, and thus cannot choose (i) 1:T for each different history encountered. arbitrarily distinct strategies of π As surrogate to quantitatively characterize Condition 2, we propose Condition 3 below, which instantiates the imperfect recall as having finite-memory with an exponential decay property. (i) Condition 3 (Exponential Decay Memory (EDM)). For given player , the strategy π 1:T of player focuses on only the latest histories due to an exponential decay memory. Formally, for any timestep [T] h, h, H, ai Ai, 1 γL(h)+1 π π (i) (ai ( h, h)) (i) (ai ( h, h)) 1 1 γL(h)+1 (3.3) for some constant γ [0, 1). Let (i) γ denote the space of all π(i) (i) that satisfy the condition. Condition 3 quantitatively bounds how sensitive the strategy is to the distant past: once the most recent suffix is fixed, changing the earlier prefix from to can only change each conditional action probability by multiplicative factor that approaches 1 at an exponential rate in the length of h, as denoted by (h). Thus, for any target accuracy ϵ > 0, it suffices to retain suffix of length = (log (1/ϵ)) to make the effect of earlier history negligible. We adopt exponential (rather than polynomial) decay precisely to ensure that this effective memory length grows only logarithmically with 1/ϵ, which ensures efficient computation. This assumption is satisfied by several regularized mirror-descent-type updates, see, e.g., Cen et al. (2021); Liu et al. (2022); Sokota et al. (2023). An alternative to quantify imperfect recall is via bounded memory (Chakraborty and Stone, 2014), where strategies depend only on the last rounds for fixed constant m. However, bounded dependence on the last observations does not prevent player from using its actions as an information carrier: by choosing actions according to protocol, the player can encode information about earlier events into the recent suffix that future decisions can still access. As shown in the proof of Lemma D.1, this phenomenon can preclude sublinear regret even when = 1. Condition 3 rules out such near-perfect state passing by forcing policies that share the same recent suffix to be nearly indistinguishable regardless of the earlier prefix, with the indistinguishability improving exponentially in the suffix length. We summarize the hardness results in Table 1 and postpone the full statements to Appendix D. Although in Table 1 we only state the necessity of Condition 2, Condition 3 is almost necessary, as the difference between these two conditions only originates from those imperfect recall strategies with the choice of γ = 1 in Condition 3 (following the convention that 1/0 = +). Given the results above, we now focus on minimizing the RP-Regret by assuming the necessary conditions summarized in Table 1 throughout, unless otherwise noted. The corresponding lemma proves that the condition is necessary. In Appendix G, we show that nonconvex optimization oracle yields sublinear RP-Regret under these necessary conditions, with Condition 2 replaced by Condition 3.

sections.title: 4 RP-Regret Minimization
sections.content: As mentioned before, unlike the traditional framework of online convex optimization with memory (Merhav et al., 2002; Anava et al., 2015), which exclusively focuses on handling convex loss functions, our loss is non-convex with respect to the input argument due to the product of strategies in consecutive timesteps. Hence, it is hard to minimize RP-Regret directly due to the non-convexity. We now propose three different ways to minimize RP-Regret as follows: Minimizing RP-Regret directly with an oracle that can optimize non-convex function, where we only need the necessary conditions in Table 1, except that we use Condition 3 instead of Condition 2. This setting with non-convex optimization oracle, though it may be computationally intractable, can still provide some insights into the players learning process, and has also been adopted in the literature (Suggala and Netrapalli, 2020). This part is presented in detail in Appendix G. Minimizing surrogate notion of RP-Regret, i.e., Local RP-Regret (LRP-Regret), by convexifying the RP-Regret locally around the implemented strategies during regret minimization, which will be discussed in 4.1. Reformulating the problem as Markov game (Shapley, 1953; Filar and Vrieze, 2012) under certain reparameterization, with an additional requirement that Condition 1 holds also for the opponent (not only for the comparator, as necessary condition by Lemma D.1). This part will be introduced in 4.2. The first bullet justifies that Condition 1 on the comparator and Condition 3 on both the comparator and the opponents are sufficient to achieve sublinear RP-Regret, when one leverages non-convex optimization oracle, as in online non-convex learning (Suggala and Netrapalli, 2020). This sufficiency result mirrors the necessity of these conditions (and their variants) in 3.2. Hereafter, we will focus on introducing the other two approaches above that account for computational efficiency.

sections.title: 4.1 Minimizing a Surrogate: Local Repeated Policy Regret

sections.title: 4.2 Minimizing RP-Regret with Slowly-Changing Opponents
sections.content: We also propose another approach that can directly minimize the original RP-Regret (3.2), under the additional condition that the opponent changes their strategies slowly, i.e., Condition 1 also holds for the opponents strategies. 9 4.2.1 Reformulating Repeated Games with Bounded Memory as Markov Games We will show that the expected loss at each timestep can be approximated by the expected loss of an infinite-horizon average-loss Markov/stochastic game (Shapley, 1953; Gillette, 1957; Filar and Vrieze, 2012), and develop regret minimization algorithm based on this reformulation. Naively, one can use the whole history as the state of the Markov game. However, the state space will increase exponentially as the timestep increases. Hence, we will consider the case where all the players have an M-bounded memory with some fixed M, such that they only make decisions conditioned on the history of length M. In Lemma L.5, we will show that under Condition 3, we can convert the unbounded memory of all players to M-bounded memory, with small approximation error. sections.title: 5 Equilibrium Computation via RP-Regret Minimization
sections.content: In this section, we investigate one important implication of our new regret notion equilibrium computation, in repeated games. We will focus on the infinitely repeated game setting, instead of the finitely repeated one. This is because, for one-shot matrix game, when its NE is unique, the only subgame perfect Nash equilibrium of its finitely repeated version is playing the NE of the one-shot game at every timestep (Benoit and Krishna, 1985). Therefore, solving the NE 7In fact, this lemma also holds when all players satisfy weaker condition (cf. Condition 5 in the Appendix). 12 of the finitely repeated game degenerates to solving the NE of the one-shot matrix game. On the other hand, the uniqueness of NE for one-shot matrix game is not uncommon for twoplayer zero-sum matrix games, the set of games with non-unique NEs has Lebesgue measure zero (Van Damme, 1991; Bailey and Piliouras, 2018). Hence, we will hereafter direct our attention to the infinitely repeated general-sum game setting.

sections.content: We now propose an algorithm to find an approximate SPCCE with O(T)-bounded deviation of infinitely repeated matrix games. The algorithm is proposed in Algorithm 3. Guarantee of the algorithm is provided below. Theorem 5.7. For an infinitely repeated game, when all the players and their comparators satisfy Condition 4 with ν (i) being the uniform strategy over Ai, within T0 N>0 iterations, the output of Algorithm 3 -approximate SPCCE with O(T)-bounded deviation of the infinitely repeated converges to an (cid:19) (cid:18) game. 1 T2/7 The proof is deferred to Section K.1. The proof relies on modeling the repeated game as Markov game as in 4.2.1, and then resorting to the existing result about solving CCE in Markov games (Jin et al., 2021; Song et al., 2022; Mao and Basar, 2022; Daskalakis et al., 2023).

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we studied regret-minimization in repeated games with adaptive opponents who can respond based on the histories of play. To this end, we advocated new metric, RP-Regret, which is native to this setting, and identified series of necessary conditions for obtaining RP-Regret sublinear in time. We then developed additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize RP-Regret, followed by the connection of RP-Regret minimization to certain known sub-game perfect equilibria computation. Our work opens new directions for future research, including the development of weaker equilibrium notions induced by RP-Regret minimization in 4, for which weaker assumptions may be required to compute. It also raises the possibility of obtaining provable equilibrium-selection results via RP-Regret minimization in games with particular payoff structures.

In each cell, the number on the left (right) is the utility for the row (column) player. The figure on the right plots the average utility across 10, 000 runs of the experiments when both players in Stag-Hunt (lower left) are minimizing LRP-Regret for 100, 000 iterations. In each run of the experiment, the initial strategy is randomly sampled uniformly. We choose the learning rate η = 0.01 in (4.3) with different memory lengths = 1, 2, 3. As shown in the figure, LRP-Regret minimization can encourage players to converge to better equilibrium in terms of utility. Example A.1 (Finding Better Equilibrium). The Stag-Hunt game (cf. utility matrix in Figure 1) has two NEs, Stag-Stag and Hare-Hare. In Figure 1, we show that by minimizing LRP-Regret (a convexified version of our new regret metric, cf. 4.1), the algorithm converges to better equilibrium more often (Stag-Stag, with utility 1.0 for both players). This is because the equilibria induced by minimizing our new notions of regret can potentially constitute larger set than the set of equilibria in the one-shot game.

sections.title: B Detailed Related Work
sections.content: Equilibrium Computation in Repeated Games. Learning and equilibrium computation in repeated normal-form games is well-studied area. One well-known folklore result is that when all the players run no-(external-)regret learning algorithms, the average iterates will converge to the coarse correlated equilibrium of the one-shot matrix game (Cesa-Bianchi and Lugosi, 2006). In playing repeated games, since the players could change their strategies based on past observations, the equilibrium for the repeated game can be different from that of the one-shot game. In this regard, using the well-known folk theorem8 (Aumann and Shapley, 1976; Fudenberg and Maskin, 2009), one can find the NE in infinitely repeated matrix games. Littman and Stone (2003) gave polynomial-time algorithm based on the folk theorem to compute the NE of an infinitely repeated two-player general-sum game, which is known to be PPAD-hard for the one-shot case (Daskalakis et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2006). However, Borgs et al. (2008) showed that finding the minimax value is NP-hard in general-sum games with 3 or more players, which prevented the technique in Littman and Stone (2003) from being extended to the multi-player cases. 8Folk theorem gives way to construct trigger strategies. Specifically, if one of the players deviates, say player 1, then other players will also change their strategy to the minimax one, to guarantee player 1 will suffer at least minπ(1) maxπ(1) L1(π(1), π(1)) loss in every later timestep. Therefore, no player can benefit from deviation if all of them can obtain lower loss than their minimax loss. 22 Impossibility Results. Borgs et al. (2008) proved that computing an NE for an N-player infinitely repeated game with discount factors is as hard as computing an NE of an (N 1)-player one-shot game. By Chen et al. (2007); Daskalakis et al. (2009); Chen et al. (2006), computing an NE of 2-player general-sum one-shot game is PPAD-hard and thus computing an NE of 3-player infinitely repeated game with discount factor is also PPAD-hard. The result does not conflict with this paper since in 5.3, we are computing coarse correlated equilibrium (CCE) instead of an NE of the repeated game. Also, our result implies that directly minimizing RP-Regret without further assumptions may not be computationally tractable. Otherwise, if each player achieves sublinear RP-Regret, by Theorem 5.4, the trajectory converges to subgame perfect NE of an infinitely repeated game without discount factor. Schlag and Zapechelnyuk (2012); Loftin and Oliehoek (2022) gave impossibility results on minimizing regret in repeated games when the regret minimizer cannot observe the full strategy of the opponent. Therefore, to the best of our knowledge, it is still unclear under which conditions we can minimize regret, when the other players deviate themselves as reaction to the deviation of one player, the setting our work focuses on. Rationalizing Cooperation. Our study is also motivated by those of rationalizing cooperation in Iterated Prisoners Dilemma with bounded rationality and strategy complexity (Axelrod and Hamilton, 1981; Neyman, 1985; Radner, 1986; Kalai and Stanford, 1988). These papers investigated the Nash equilibrium of the (finitely) repeated game when the strategies are modeled as an automaton with finite states, and showed the existence of equilibrium with payoff close to the cooperative outcome. In this paper, we show, in contrast, that when players have unlimited memory power (automata with infinite states), our regret notion of RP-Regret cannot be minimized. Opponent Shaping and Modeling. Our work also takes significant inspiration from the recent empirical literature of opponent shaping in multi-agent (reinforcement) learning (Foerster et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2021; Willi et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2022), which developed algorithms to shape the opponents future strategies, knowing that they will adapt to the actions of the learning agent/player. However, most of the algorithms do not enjoy theoretical guarantees, e.g., on the convergence, and/or on the quality of the strategies they converge to. Letcher et al. (2019) proved that their algorithm called Stable Opponent Shaping will converge. However, the guarantee only applies when assuming the opponents are naive learners9, and there is no guarantee on the performance against general unknown opponents. By contrast, our method, through the lens of regret-minimization, is more general and robust against any possible players/opponents satisfying some provably necessary conditions (see 3.2). Online Learning. Our work is closely related to the problem of online convex optimization with memory, which was first studied in Merhav et al. (2002) and later generalized to developing the concept of Policy Regret and studied in Arora et al. (2012); Anava et al. (2015). Recently, Zhao et al. (2022) showed how to achieve sublinear policy regret when the comparator is allowed to choose time-varying strategy with sublinear variation budget. One of the applications of OCO with memory is Agarwal et al. (2019), which discussed how to fit the online control problem into the framework of OCO with memory. However, existing analyses along these lines typically either assume that the loss function is convex or impose an m-bounded-memory condition on the loss, namely that the loss at timestep depends only on the recent action window [atm, . . . , at]. In contrast, in repeated games the expected per-round loss induced by strategic interaction need 9At each timestep, the learner takes one step of gradient descent according to the loss at the last timestep. 23 not be convex in the players decision variables, and it can depend on the entire history. Blocki et al. (2013) considered regret minimization in bounded-memory games. However, the k-adaptive regret considered in Blocki et al. (2013) will restart the game every + 1 rounds (i.e., the adversary will forget the history every + 1 rounds), which is different setting from classical repeated games, where the adversary plays repeated games and will remember the history from the beginning to the end. Another line of related work in online learning is dynamic regret minimization (Zinkevich, 2003). In this setup, the accumulated loss is compared with comparator that can change over time, but usually with sublinear variation budget. Therefore, dynamic regret is more suitable when the environment is non-stationary. To encourage the players strategy to adapt to the changes in the opponents strategies, we also consider dynamic comparators, but in game-theoretic settings, and with different regret notions. Proof for Example 1. In Iterated Prisoners Dilemma, since the players are symmetric, we will focus on analyzing player 1 without loss of generality. Note that tit-for-tat (where both players start with C) promotes cooperation, since the players will stick to and the time-average utility will be 0.6, higher than that of the one-shot Nash equilibrium (D, D). However, tit-for-tat may not be good strategy in terms of external regret: mutually playing it will cause linear external regret, because the regret of player 1 when compared to always playing is (1.0 0.6)T. In fact, for any player that achieves sublinear external regret in IPD for any T, the time-average strategy must converge to as , since otherwise the player will suffer linear regret compared to the fixed action D, the strictly dominant strategy. On the other hand, for the RP-Regret defined in (3.2), consider the first timestep that player 1 is going to deviate from deterministically choosing (since the beginning of playing tit-fortat mutually) to some comparator strategy. In this case, since the expected utility is multilinear function of the comparator strategy, and the comparator is in the Cartesian product of each timesteps strategy space, there exists an optimal deviation strategy of RP-Regret in the comparator space that is deterministic choosing either or at every timestep. Furthermore, at this timestep when the comparator is different from deterministically choosing for the first time, we can restrict the comparator strategy to be an oblivious (in addition to deterministic) one, since the history h1:t1 has always been (C, C) before, deterministically. With tit-for-tat opponent, their strategy is deterministic at timestep + 1 as well. By induction, with such deterministic history, it does not lose optimality to restrict the comparator at + 1 to be oblivious as well. Next, we will show that at timestep t, the best deviation for the comparator is actually C. Then, by induction, the comparator should choose deterministically at all timesteps except possibly at the last timestep = T. Suppose the comparator at timestep is going to play action with some probability and with probability 1 w, then they will receive an expected utility of u1(C, C) + (1 w) u1(D, C) = 0.6w + (1 w) = 1 0.4w, where u1(a1, a2) denotes the utility of player 1 when the actions of both players are a1, a2, respectively. When < T, for timestep + 1, suppose the comparator will play action with probability and with probability 1 w. Now, player 2, still following tit-for-tat, changes from playing deterministically at timestep t, to playing with probability w, due to the deviation of player 1 at timestep t. Therefore, the expected utility of 24 player 1 at timestep + 1 now is ww u1(C, C) + w(1 w) u1(D, C) + (1 w)w u1(C, D) + (1 w)(1 w) u1(D, D) = 0.8w 0.2ww 0.2w + 0.2. The summation of the expected utility of player 1 at timestep and + 1 is thus 0.4w 0.2ww 0.2w + 1.2 = 1.2 0.2w + w(0.4 0.2w). Note that we analyze the deviation at timestep by only looking at the expected utility at these two timesteps, since will not affect the utility after + 1 (if exists), given player 2 following tit-for-tat. Noting that 1, we know 0.4 0.2w > 0, and further have that should be 1.0 in order to maximize the summed expected utility (regardless of the choice of w). Hence, the comparator should not deviate from at this timestep t. Continuing the induction, we further know that the comparator has no incentive to deviate from for < T, until = T. However, this will at most increase the utility by 1.0 0.6 = 0.4, leading to constant (and thus sublinear) RP-Regret in terms of T. The key ingredient that differentiates these two regret notions is that RP-Regret considers the influence of deviation at the current timestep on future timesteps, by accounting for the adaptivity of the opponents, while external regret assumes that the opponents are non-adaptive. Remark C.1. In fact, the argument above is also aligned with the known result that tit-for-tat is an NE in an infinitely repeated Prisoners Dilemma: since we proved that deviating from tit-for-tat is beneficial only when = T, such final timestep is nonexistent in the infinitely repeated setting. Hence, there is in effect no incentive to deviate.

D.1.1 Proofs of Results in Table 1 Proof of Lemma D.1. Consider two-player coin-flipping game shown in Table 2. The opponent (the column player) can flip the coin to any side they want, and player 1 (the row player) needs to guess which side the coin is, with loss 0 for correct guess and loss 1 for wrong guess. For any strategy sequence of player 1, at each timestep, there exists side that player 1 guesses with probability no larger than 0.5. Let the column player deterministically flip to that side. This fixes an oblivious deterministic opponent sequence against which player 1 incurs expected loss at least 0.5 at every timestep. Without Condition 1 on the comparator, there exists comparator that deterministically guesses this fixed sequence correctly every time, so that the corresponding accumulated loss is 0, while player 1s accumulated loss under any strategy sequence is no less than 0.5T. Hence, player 1 will get linear RP-Regret. In this case, both the opponent and the comparator only use deterministic strategies, and are oblivious in the sense that at timestep t, they do not depend on the history before t. Suppose Condition 3 is satisfied for all players. The expected time-average loss of the induced Markov game defined in Definition 4.2 always exists and does not depend on the initial distribution. Proof. Fix any player . Without loss of generality, we may restrict attention to policies π(i) that have full support conditioned on every history. Indeed, by Condition 3, if π(i) (ai h) = 0 for some ai Ai and H, then necessarily π(i) (ai h) = 0 for all H. In this case, action ai is never taken (from any history), so we can remove ai from Ai without changing the limiting time-average loss, because any state that involves ai is transient and has zero probability of being visited on average in the limit. Moreover, if every player uses policy with full support conditional on any history, then every joint action is selected with positive probability whenever it is available. Consequently, the induced Markov chain is irreducible. Therefore, the time-average loss exists and is independent of the initial distribution (Norris, 1998). Lemma L.5.
